coredata.dc:title: Sensitive fluorescence detection for hydrogen peroxide and glucose using biomass carbon dots: Dual-quenching mechanism insight
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Lei
Pan, Wenyue
Ye, Heng
Liang, Ning
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, blue-emitting chicken cartilage carbon dots (cc-CDs) were synthesized using biomass as green precursors. The abundant heteroatoms in biomass allowed for self-doping during the synthesis of cc-CDs. The experimental results showed that cc-CDs possessed excellent optical properties and good stability. The elemental composition, graphitized structure and surface groups of cc-CDs have characterized by various instruments. In addition, the low cytotoxicity of cc-CDs was demonstrated by the high viability of cells which incubated with cc-CDs solutions. Subsequently, a sensitive and accurate biosensor for the determination of H2O2 was established using cc-CDs as fluorescent probe. In a departure from previous reports, fluorescence quenching was confirmed to be caused by the dual effects of Fe3+ and hydroxyl radicals rather than the single effect of Fe3+. The method for detection of H2O2 exhibited good selectivity, wide linear range (2–500 μM, 500–1500 μM) and low detection limit (0.47 μM). In addition, the biosensor could be used to detect glucose in the presence of glucose oxidase (GOx). The sensing system was further applied to the detection of glucose in human serum, and satisfactory results were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose
Detection of glucose in real sample
The construction and working mechanism of the sensing system
Fluorescent response and selectivity of cc-CDs to H2O2
Fluorescent response and interference of cc-CDs to glucose
Detecting glucose in human serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University ( ZQN2016011 ), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department ( 2020LZD02 ), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province ( 2020-181 ), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM images of cc-CDs. (B) Particle size distribution of cc-CDs. (C) XRD spectrum of the cc-CDs. (D) Fluorescence maximum excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of cc-CDs and UV absorption spectra of cc-CDs (green solid line), Fe3+ (orange dotted line) and Fe2+/H2O2 (blue dotted line). (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of cc-CDs for various excitation wavelengths. (F) FTIR spectrum of the as-prepared cc-CDs.
(A) Full XPS spectrum of cc-CDs. High resolution spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D).

Fluorescence spectra (A) Mixed systems of different substances and cc-CDs. (B) The effect of tert-butanol on the fluorescence value of three systems. The UV absorption spectra (C) and fluorescence lifetime curves (D) of cc-CDs, cc-CDs/Fe3+ and cc-CDs/Fe2+/H2O2.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of cc-CDs/Fe2+/H2O2 with H2O2 concentration in the range of 2–3000 μM. (B) Fluorescence spectra of cc-CDs/Fe2+/Gox/glucose with glucose concentration in the range of 5–3000 μM. (C) The linear relationship between fluorescence value and H2O2 concentration in the range of 2–500 μM and 500–1500 μM. (D) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and glucose concentration in the range of 5–1000 μM. (E) The effects of H2O2 and interferents on fluorescence intensity of cc-CDs/Fe2+ system. (F) The effects of interferents on fluorescence intensity of cc-CDs/Fe2+/glucose/GOx system.
Cell viability of three kinds of cells treated with different concentrations of cc-CDs solution for 24 h.
Analytical performance of the methods reported for H2O2 detection.
Analytical performances of the methods reported for glucose detection.
Determination of glucose in real serum samples by this method (n = 6).

head.ce:title: Sensitive fluorescence detection for hydrogen peroxide and glucose using biomass carbon dots: Dual-quenching mechanism insight

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical Engineering, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016 Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, blue-emitting chicken cartilage carbon dots (cc-CDs) were synthesized using biomass as green precursors. The abundant heteroatoms in biomass allowed for self-doping during the synthesis of cc-CDs. The experimental results showed that cc-CDs possessed excellent optical properties and good stability. The elemental composition, graphitized structure and surface groups of cc-CDs have characterized by various instruments. In addition, the low cytotoxicity of cc-CDs was demonstrated by the high viability of cells which incubated with cc-CDs solutions. Subsequently, a sensitive and accurate biosensor for the determination of H2O2 was established using cc-CDs as fluorescent probe. In a departure from previous reports, fluorescence quenching was confirmed to be caused by the dual effects of Fe3+ and hydroxyl radicals rather than the single effect of Fe3+. The method for detection of H2O2 exhibited good selectivity, wide linear range (2–500 μM, 500–1500 μM) and low detection limit (0.47 μM). In addition, the biosensor could be used to detect glucose in the presence of glucose oxidase (GOx). The sensing system was further applied to the detection of glucose in human serum, and satisfactory results were obtained.

The dual-quenching effects on the fluorescence of cc-CDs was derived from Fe3+ and ·OH.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a kind of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and an important biomolecule produced by the human body [1]. The level of H2O2 in the human body is closely related to various diseases such as diabetes, cancer, Alzheimer's, Parkinson's and Huntington's diseases [2–5]. Many substances in the human body can be catalytically oxidized to produce H2O2, such as glucose, uric acid and xanthine [6–8]. As the main fuel for glycolysis and the downstream pathway of aerobic as well as anaerobic respiration, glucose is one of the most important elements of human health [9–11]. Considering the influence of the levels of H2O2 and glucose on human health, it is extremely necessary to establish a sensitive, rapid and accurate method for quantifying H2O2 and glucose.

The common method for glucose detection is to detect H2O2 produced through the catalytic oxidation of glucose by glucose oxidase (GOx) [12]. Compared with conventional glucose oxidase-peroxidase colorimetry, the fluorescence analysis based on carbon dots (CDs) is regarded as a potential method and has received great attention from researchers because of its simple operation, high sensitivity, good cost-effectiveness and fast response speed [13–15]. CDs are novel and fascinating zero-dimensional carbon-based materials with particle size of less than 10 nm [6,16]. With unique optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, good water solubility and easy surface functionalization, CDs have been widely used in fields of fluorescence sensing, bioimaging and drug delivery [17–19].

In the detection of H2O2, the coexistence of H2O2 and Fe2+ lead to the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots. Fluorescence quenching is a promising form for constructing analyte detection methods. The main forms of fluorescence quenching are static and dynamic quenching [20]. The lifetime of excited-state molecules is shortened in dynamic quenching, which is the most important difference from static quenching [21]. Fluorescence quenching can originate from collision quenching, energy/electron transfer, molecular rearrangement, excited state reaction, ground state complex formation and luminescence structure destruction [22]. Previous reports mostly believed that the fluorescence quenching in the method was due to the formation of ground state complexes between Fe3+ and CDs [20,23,24]. However, after a careful study of the detection mechanism, we found that the quenching was not only the result of the single effect of Fe3+.

Herein, we first prepared chicken cartilage carbon dots (cc-CDs) with blue fluorescence using food-derived precursors. Glucose can be converted to H2O2. We proved that the fluorescence quenching of cc-CDs was attributed to the dual effects of Fe3+ and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) produced by the coexistence of H2O2 and Fe2+. On this basis, a highly sensitive and convenient "turn-off" fluorescent probe method for detecting H2O2 and glucose has been successfully established. Moreover, the low cytotoxicity of the prepared cc-CDs was verified by cell experiments. The method was low in cost, environmentally friendly, good biocompatibility, and could realize the quantitative analysis of glucose in actual serum samples.

All the chemicals used were of at least analytic grade and without any treatment before use. The solutions were prepared with deionized water throughout the experiment. The test metal ions, anions and glucose used were purchased from Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). Fructose, sucrose, maltose, lactose and urea were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). Glucose oxidase (GOx), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GSH) were supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Finally, chicken cartilage was purchased from a local supermarket (Shenyang, China).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded by JEM 2100 high resolution TEM. Fluorescence spectra were obtained from F97 (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd. China). UV-5500PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Metash Instruments, China) was used to record UV–vis absorption spectra. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was obtained from D8-Focus X-ray diffractometer (Brook, German). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of cc-CDs were characterized by an Escalab250Xi (Thermo Fisher, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on A Nicolet IS spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) to analysis surface functional groups. Time resolved fluorescence spectrometer (PLS 980, Edinburgh, UK) was applied to measure fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield. In addition, a microplate reader (Varioskan LUX Thermo Fisher, USA) was used to record cytotoxicity data. Cell images were filmed by fully automatic live cell microscopic imaging system (Evos FL Auto2, Thermo Fisher, USA).

The chicken cartilage was used as a green carbon source for the synthesis of cc-CDs, which were prepared through simple hydrothermal carbonization, filtration and dialysis. Initially, the washed chicken cartilage was dehydrated in a drying oven and then smashed into fine fibers. Thereafter, 0.2 g of fine fibers were added into 20 mL of deionized water and dispersed under ultrasonication for 10 min before transferred the sample mixture into Teflon-lined autoclaves (50 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The product in the autoclave was naturally cooled down. The upper brown solution was first filtered with a 0.22 µm filter membrane, and then the filtrate was dialyzed with deionized water for 24 h to obtain cc-CDs. The resulting pure cc-CDs solution was freeze-dried or preserved at − 4 °C for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The detection process of H2O2 was as follows: Firstly, 300 µL of 600 µg mL−1 cc-CDs aqueous solution and various concentrations of H2O2 (200 µL) were mixed with 1.5 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10 mM, pH = 3.5). Then 200 µL of 3 mM FeSO4 solution was added into the obtained solution. The resulting solution was diluted with deionized water to 3 mL before the solution was reacted at room temperature for 10 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra and intensity at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm were recorded.

The detection process of glucose: The content of glucose was indirectly reflected by the content of H2O2. Firstly, various concentrations of glucose were incubated with GOx (0.5 mg mL−1) in PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.2) at 40 °C for 40 min to generate H2O2. The other steps were the same as for the measurement of H2O2.

The effects of cc-CDs on cell viability of cell lines including human colon cancer (HCT 116), mouse cardiomyocytes (MC) and murine hepatoma (Hepa 1–6) were determined by CCK-8 assay. In brief, these three types of cells were plated in a 96-well plate with 104 cells per well and cultured 24 h at 37 °C incubator containing 5% CO2. Then 100 µL cc-CDs solutions of different concentrations (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100 and 200 µg mL−1) were added to each well to treat the cell for 24 h (repeated 6 groups for each concentration). Subsequently, 10 µL of CCK-8 reagent was added into each well and incubated with the cells for 2–3 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The human serum comes from healthy volunteers. The serum samples should be centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min in advance to obtain the supernatant. The supernatant was diluted 50 times with deionized water, and then various concentrations of glucose solution were added to it. Finally, the above method was used to analyze the recovery rate of the glucose content.

The recorded HRTEM image showed that cc-CDs were roughly spherical and well-dispersed in aqueous solution, and its average particle size was 7.6 nm ( Fig. 1A, B). The prepared cc-CDs demonstrated high crystallinity with lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction surface of graphitic (sp2) carbon (Fig. 1A) [25]. In Fig. 1C, XRD spectrum showed an intense diffraction peak located at 2θ = 23.2°, which corresponded to the planes of graphite lattice spacing (002). The above results revealed the highly crystalline and graphite structure of the synthesized cc-CDs. The UV absorption characteristics, maximum excitation spectrum and corresponding emission spectrum of the obtained cc-CDs were shown in Fig. 1D. The two UV-Vis absorption peaks at 252 and 330 nm were ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds in the graphitized carbon core and n-π* transition of CO bonds on the surface of cc-CDs, respectively. The prepared cc-CDs displayed blue fluorescence under excitation at 310–380 nm wavelength. As the excitation wavelength increased, the maximum emission wavelength was red-shifted, which showed the characteristic of excitation light dependence (Fig. 1E). The excitation light-dependent behavior of cc-CDs was caused by the different sizes and surface defects of the nanoparticles, which was the same as that of most light-emitting cc-CDs and graphene quantum dots [26]. And at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the maximum emission of 435 nm was obtained. In addition, it was detected that the absolute photoluminescence quantum yield of the prepared cc-CDs at a wavelength of 360 nm was 10.3% (Details are in Electronic Supplementary Material).

The functional groups on the surface of cc-CDs were identified by FTIR spectrum. As shown in Fig. 1F, the strong and broad near-infrared absorption at 3200–3500 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of -OH and N-H on the surface of cc-CDs. Two peaks at 1384 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibration of C-N and CO bonds. In addition, the peak at 1106 cm−1 was ascribed to the presence of C-O-C.

To further confirm the analysis results of FTIR, XPS was used to determine the element types and valence states of cc-CDs. Full XPS of the prepared cc-CDs showed that the three element peaks at 284.7, 399.5 and 531.1 eV were designated as C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively ( Fig. 2A). In the high-resolution XPS spectrum, C1s band could be divided into four peaks (Fig. 2B), corresponding to CO (287.9 eV), C-O-C (286.7 eV), C-N (285.6 eV) and C-C/CC (284.4 eV), respectively, indicating that a large number of unsaturated carbon bonds were formed during the hydrothermal carbonization process. The two peaks of N1s band at 399.1 and 400.7 eV indicated that most of the nitrogen elements existed in the form of graphite N and amino N (Fig. 2C) [27]. The O1s spectrum displayed two peaks at 531.1 and 532.8 eV, representing to O-C and OC, respectively (Fig. 2D). The above results were consistent with the FTIR results. The presence of graphitized carbon core and N-doping was responsible for the strong fluorescence. The good water solubility and hydrophilicity were due to the widespread hydrophilic groups on the surface of cc-CDs.

The stability of cc-CDs has been investigated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of cc-CDs under various pH values, ion concentrations, long-term UV irradiation and difference temperatures. In the range of pH from 2 to 7, the change of fluorescence intensity was not obvious, and there was a maximum emission intensity at pH = 3.95 (Fig. S1A). As shown in Fig. S1B, the fluorescence emission peak was slightly changed when irradiated by an ultraviolet lamp for 90 min, indicating that cc-CDs had satisfactory light stability. In Fig. S1C, with the increase of NaCl concentrations, the fluorescence intensity changed little and gradually flattened out, indicating that cc-CDs possessed a certain tolerance to high ionic strength. In Fig. S1D, the probe solution showed stable fluorescence intensity at different temperatures, indicating that the prepared cc-CDs were stable in a certain range of temperatures. In summary, the prepared cc-CDs had excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The construction and working mechanism of the sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: It can be seen from Fig. 3A, compared with Fe3+，adding Fe2+ or H2O2 alone had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of cc-CDs. This phenomenon could be attributed to the fact that the affinity of hydroxyl groups on the surface of carbon core to Fe3+ was much higher than Fe2+, and there was no ester group to recognize H2O2. The mixture of H2O2 and Fe2+ could quench the fluorescence intensity of cc-CDs more significantly than Fe3+. This indicated that ·OH produced by the Fenton reaction also played a role in the fluorescence quenching process of cc-CDs. In addition, GOx can catalyze the oxidation of glucose to H2O2. Accordingly, we could build a sensor platform for measuring glucose using H2O2 as a bridge. The working processes of the analysis include: (1) Glucose was catalytically oxidized by GOx to generate H2O2. (2) The reaction of H2O2 and Fe2+ produced Fe3+ and ·OH. (3) The decrease in the fluorescence intensity of cc-CDs was due to the dual actions of ·OH and Fe3+.

Subsequently, we explored the different quenching mechanisms of Fe3+ and ·OH. The fluorescence response, linear relationship and selectivity of cc-CDs to Fe3+ have been studied (Fig. S2). The non-specific complexation between Fe3+ and the functional groups on the surface of cc-CDs, including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, could cause fluorescence quenching [28]. It can be seen from the UV spectra (Fig. 3C) that the absorption peaks changed obviously in the presence of Fe3+, indicating that a non-luminescent complex generated [20]. In Fig. 3D, the fluorescence lifetime curves were fitted with the bi-exponential decay function. The fluorescence lifetime of cc-CDs and cc-CDs/Fe3+ were 9.76 ns and 9.68 ns, respectively. The insignificant change in fluorescence lifetime confirmed that the process of fluorescence quenching due to Fe3+ was static. In addition, Fig. 1D showed the absorption peaks of Fe3+ and Fe2+/H2O2 overlapped with the excitation spectrum of cc-CDs, which provided the possibility for the inner-filter effect. Therefore, the quenching of cc-CDs by Fe3+ was presumably attributed to a static quenching process combined with inner-filter effect.

Then, tert-butanol was added to the cc-CDs/Fe2+/H2O2 system to quench ·OH (Fig. 3B). It can be found that there was no significant difference between the fluorescence intensity of the sample contained tert-butanol and cc-CDs/Fe3+, which showed that ·OH also played an indispensable role in the quenching process. In Fig. 3D, the fluorescence lifetime of cc-CDs and cc-CDs/Fe2+/H2O2 were 9.76 ns and 7.68 ns, respectively. The decrease of fluorescence lifetime was an obvious feature of dynamic quenching. Furthermore, in Fig. 1D, the absorption spectrum of Fe2+/H2O2 hardly overlapped with the emission spectrum of cc-CDs, which excluded the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer. As we all known, ·OH has a super high oxidation potential, which can destroy the chemical structure of organic dyes. We believed that it was the highly reactive ·OH that destroyed the emission groups of cc-CDs, which caused the fluorescence from the carbon nucleus to be effectively quenched [29]. As shown in Fig. 3C, the absorption peak of the cc-CDs/Fe2+/H2O2 system was lower than that of the cc-CDs/Fe3+ system. This phenomenon was probably due to the destruction of partial groups on the surface of cc-CDs, which reduced the degree of complexation between cc-CDs and Fe3+. In addition, the destruction of this conjugated structure was also shown in the fluorescence spectra as an obvious blue-shifting of the peak and a decrease in intensity (Fig. 3A) [30]. In short, strong quenching was the result of the dual effects of Fe3+ and ·OH, which also made the determination of H2O2 more sensitive.

First, the synthesis temperature and time of cc-CDs were optimized (Fig. S3A). The fluorescence values enhanced with the increases of temperature and time. However, when it reached 8 h, the enhancement degree of fluorescence value was lower by continuing to increase the time. The reaction at 200 °C for 8 h was selected as the final synthesis condition based on energy saving and safety considerations. In addition, to explore the optimal detection conditions, important experimental parameters affecting the detection of H2O2, including Fe2+ concentration, temperature, reaction time and pH were optimized. The optimal amount of GOx (0.5 mg mL−1) for glucose detection was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S3B, as the Fe2+ concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity of cc-CDs/Fe2+/H2O2 system dropped sharply and it remained stable after the concentration of Fe2+ reached 200 µM. In Fig. S3C, there was no significant difference in the fluorescence intensity of the cc-CDs/Fe2+/H2O2 system at different temperatures, which proved that the cc-CDs/Fe2+/H2O2 system was not sensitive to temperature. Therefore, room temperature was chosed as the reaction temperature. Fig. S3D illustrated that the fluorescence intensity of the system no longer changed significantly after the reaction time reached 10 min. Thus 10 min was selected as the reaction time for subsequent experiments. As for pH, it was known from the above that the fluorescence intensity of cc-CDs had a maximum value at pH 3–4 (Fig. S1A). And the fluorescence of the system had the largest quenching degree at pH = 3.5 (Fig. S3E). Therefore, the PBS buffer solution (pH = 3.5) was used for further analysis. Furthermore, the enzyme activity of GOx for glucose detection was shown in Fig. S3F. 20 U enzyme activity was considered to be the best experimental level and applied to glucose detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response and selectivity of cc-CDs to H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized experimental conditions, the fluorescence of the system was gradually quenched with the increase of H2O2 concentration and the degree of quenching was visible to the naked eye ( Fig. 4A). The fluorescence intensity possessed a good linear relationship with H2O2 in the concentration range of 2–500 µM and 500–1500 µM. The fitted linear equations were y = −0.3948x + 488.49 (R2 = 0.9938) and y = − 0.1118x + 350.48 (R2 = 0.9898), respectively, and the detection limit was 0.47 µM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 4C).

The selectivity and interference investigations were extremely important contents in the feasibility study of the method. The selectivity of cc-CDs to H2O2 was carefully analysed by compared with common ions (Ca2+, K+, Mn2+, Mg2+, F-, Cl-, CO3 2-, SO4 2- and NO2 -), urea, sugars (Fructose, Sucrose, Maltose, Lactose and Glutamate), amino acids (Histidine, Glycine and Threonine) and endogenous substances (UA, AA and GSH). The interference substances added to the cc-CDs/Fe2+ system did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the system (Fig. 4E). The cc-CDs/Fe2+ system has been confirmed to have good selectivity to H2O2. In summary, the proposed fluorescence method could detect H2O2 sensitively and possessed excellent performance compared with the reported method ( Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response and interference of cc-CDs to glucose
ce:section.ce:para: First, glucose and GOx were incubated under the best enzyme activity to produce H2O2, and then the cc-CDs/Fe2+ system was added to quench the fluorescence of the system. Fig. 4B showed the fluorescent peaks of the cc-CDs gradually decreased with the increase of glucose concentrations. The fluorescence intensity exhibited a good linear relationship with the glucose concentration in the range of 5–1000 μM. The fitted linear equation was y = −0.1908x + 432.30 (R2 = 0.9969), and the detection limit was 1.22 µM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 4D).

The specific recognition of glucose by GOx made this method deservedly highly selective for glucose, so it was more meaningful to investigate the interference of substances on glucose determination. At the same time, the anti-interference ability of H2O2 detection was also tested because the method used H2O2 as the medium. As shown in Fig. 4F, it was worth noting that although AA and GSH had effects on the fluorescence value of the sensing system, the levels of endogenous AA and GSH were much lower than glucose (millimolar) in normal human serum [37–39]. Moreover, in this study, the established sensing system was suitable for the analysis of glucose at micromolar level. Therefore, the samples must be properly diluted before measuring glucose in real serum. The dilution of serum samples reduced the endogenous AA and GSH contents, thus reducing the interference. Therefore, the effects of extremely low levels of AA and GSH on the determination of glucose could be tolerated and accepted. Consequently, the established sensing system possessed satisfactory anti-interference ability. The proposed fluorescence method for detecting glucose had superior performance compared with the reported method ( Table 2).

Low or non-toxicity is the gold standard for evaluating materials used in biosensors. The standard CCK-8 method was used to detect the survival rate of three kinds of cells, including HCT 116, MC and Hepa 1–6, incubated with different concentrations of cc-CDs solutions for 24 h to evaluate the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of the prepared cc-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 5, the three types of cells maintained more than 80% cell viability after 24 h of treatment with various concentrations of cc-CDs solution. Moreover, in Fig. S4, it could be clearly observed that the cells were in good morphologies before and after been processed and there was no obvious cell damage. In short, the prepared cc-CDs not only had unique optical properties, but also showed relatively low toxicity, which were suitable for further research and application in the cellular environment and clinical medicine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting glucose in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: We performed recovery experiments on spiked samples to demonstrate the potential application of the established method in actual samples. It was worth noting that the original Fe2+ in human serum may interfere with blood glucose detection. Generally speaking, iron in human serum rarely present in the form of ions, and the content of Fe2+ is about 7–14 μM. Due to the dilution of the serum before the determination, the content was much lower than the amount of Fe2+ added in the system (200 μM), thus the background interference of Fe2+ in the serum could be ignored. The test results were shown in Table 3. The average recovery rate of glucose was between 98.1% and 102.2%, and the relative standard deviation was less than 5% (n = 6). The above results confirmed the potential applicability of the constructed biosensor for detecting glucose in serum samples.

In summary, we synthesized a green, water-soluble and highly stable fluorescent cc-CDs using food-derived chicken cartilage. A multi-analyte determination method for Fe3+, H2O2 and glucose was established. Different from the past, dual quenching was proposed to explain the mechanism of the method. Compared with the reported method, it showed excellent performance including wide linear range, high sensitivity and selectivity. In addition, the cytotoxicity test displayed that cc-CDs did not cause significant cell damage even at high concentration (200 µg mL−1), indicating that the prepared cc-CDs possessed low cytotoxicity. The application of this method to the determination of glucose in actual serum samples had obtained satisfactory results. This method for the detection of glucose used H2O2 as a “bridge”. It was reasonable to believe that this method could be extended to the analysis of other H2O2 related precursors, such as uric acid, xanthine, etc. Interestingly, we attributed the mechanism of fluorescence quenching by ·OH to the destruction of the cc-CDs structure, which may imply that low-toxic food-derived cc-CDs were a promising candidate for free radical scavenging or anti-oxidation.

Lei Wu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Software, Writing – original draft. Wenyue Pan: Formal analysis, Software. Heng Ye: Data curation. Ning Liang: Writing – review & editing. Longshan Zhao: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.128330.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
